model.label: Kimi K2.5
model.description: Moonshot AI • Reasoning model from the Kimi family built for complex thinking tasks.

pick.rationale: Duke holds an overwhelming statistical advantage as the #1 ranked team nationally with a historic +39.17 adjusted efficiency margin that far exceeds UConn's metrics. The Blue Devils' #1 ranked defense presents a severe mismatch against UConn's 54th-ranked offense, while Duke's top-5 offense can exploit gaps in the Huskies' good-but-not-elite defense. Although UConn possesses championship pedigree under Dan Hurley, their recent struggles—including losses to Marquette and a blowout defeat to St. John's in the Big East tournament—combined with Duke's demonstrated dominance even without injured starters Caleb Foster and Patrick Ngongba II, provide a reliable path for the top overall seed to advance to the Final Four.

reasoningStep.title: Elite 8 East: Duke vs UConn Statistical Evaluation
reasoningStep.summary: Comprehensive analysis of efficiency metrics, injury status, and recent form reveals Duke holds a commanding 15-20 point efficiency margin advantage with the nation's #1 defense and #4 offense. UConn's 24th-ranked defense and 54th-ranked offense face severe mismatches, while recent losses to Marquette and St. John's highlight vulnerability against elite competition.
evidence: Duke: #1 KenPom, +39.17 efficiency margin, #1 defense, #4 offense
UConn: 12th SRS, 24th defense, 54th offense, recent losses to Marquette and St. John's
Duke dominated ACC Tournament without injured starters Foster and Ngongba II
UConn's Jaylin Stewart available but team has struggled in March

result.text: UConn's Dan Hurley gives injury updates before NCAA Tournament Stamford Advocate Logo Hearst Newspapers Logo Skip to main content Subscribe Home Contact the Advocate Newsletters Advertise with Us Terms of Use Archives FAQ Privacy Notice Submissions News Health Education Politics Election Sports GametimeCT UConn Pro Baseball Pro Football Pro Basketball Pro Hockey Business Top Workplaces Real Estate Entertainment Comics Living Food Opinion Best Of Education Home Services Legal Services Senior Living Entertainment Food & Drink Health Obituaries Puzzles Advertising Features Events Jobs Classifieds Stamford Advocate Logo Subscribe Sports Dan Hurley gives injury updates on UConn's Jaylin Stewart, Silas Demary Jr. ahead of NCAA Tournament By Declan Walsh , Staff Writer March 16, 2026 STORRS, CONNECTICUT - NOVEMBER 19: Silas Demary Jr. #2 and Jaylin Stewart #3 of the Connecticut Huskies dive for the loose ball against Brayden Burries #5 of the Arizona Wildcats during the first half of an NCAA men's basketball game. Joe Buglewicz/Getty Images The UConn men's basketball team prepares for the upcoming NCAA Tournament potentially low on manpower with the injury statuses of two key contributors still in question ahead of Friday's opener against Furman. Jaylin Stewart has not played since Feb. 21, as the junior wing continues to recover from an unspecified knee injury, while Silas Demary Jr. was forced to exit Saturday's Big East Tournament final with a left ankle sprain . Dan Hurley provided updates on both players while speaking with the media Sunday evening. Advertisement Article continues below this ad "Health-wise, it will be close with Jaylin," Hurley said. The UConn coach provided a more promising update on his star point guard: "I think Silas is in pretty good shape." Jaylin Stewart has faced a mercurial junior season, starting UConn's first 10 games this season as freshman wing Braylon Mullins battled a pre-season injury — the Seattle native delivered double-digit scoring performances against Arizona and Texas. Stewart returned to an off-the-bench role once Mullins hit his stride around the New Year, but he stepped up with a crucial pair of 3-pointers against Villanova in his final game before the ongoing injury saga. More For You Here's what to know about the CIAC Division III boys hockey championship No. 10 E.O. Smith/Tolland takes on No. 5 Hall for the D-III boys hockey crown Thursday at 7 p.m. at Quinnipiac. UConn's Serah Williams set for her first NCAA Tournament appearance After 123 games and 1,720 points scored, Williams will be in her first career NCAA Tournament game. STORRS, CONNECTICUT - FEBRUARY 18: Jaylin Stewart #3 of the Connecticut Huskies gets the rebound against Hudson Greer #10 of the Creighton Bluejays during the first half of an NCAA men's basketball game at Harry A. Gampel Pavilion on February 18, 2026 in Storrs, Connecticut. (Photo by Joe Buglewicz/Getty Images) Joe Bug

You are an analyst predicting a single March Madness game inside a bracket run.
You have live tools. Use them before making a pick when they can reduce uncertainty.
You are not filling the whole bracket in one response. Predict only the current game.
Use prior picks as already committed bracket state.
You may use at most 10 tool rounds before you must finalize your answer.
Before making a pick, investigate relevant context such as injuries or availability concerns, recent team news, prior head-to-head results when useful, and multiple raw statistics from the available ratings and matchup data.
Take a holistic approach. Balance team quality, matchup specifics, health, form, schedule context, and upset risk instead of relying on one metric.
Do not force an upset, but explicitly consider whether the underdog has a credible path to win and let that affect confidence and rationale.
Return strict JSON only.
Final JSON keys: pick, reasoningStep.
pick must contain gameId, winnerId, confidence, rationale.
The rationale must be a short paragraph of 2 to 4 sentences explaining why the winner was chosen.
reasoningStep must contain id, title, summary, evidence.

Jaylin Stewart has faced a mercurial junior season, starting UConn's first 10 games this season as freshman wing Braylon Mullins battled a pre-season injury — the Seattle native delivered double-digit scoring performances against Arizona and Texas. Stewart returned to an off-the-bench role once Mullins hit his stride around the New Year, but he stepped up with a crucial pair of 3-pointers against Villanova in his final game before the ongoing injury saga. More For You Here's what to know about the CIAC Division III boys hockey championship No. 10 E.O. Smith/Tolland takes on No. 5 Hall for the D-III boys hockey crown Thursday at 7 p.m. at Quinnipiac. UConn's Serah Williams set for her first NCAA Tournament appearance After 123 games and 1,720 points scored, Williams will be in her first career NCAA Tournament game. STORRS, CONNECTICUT - FEBRUARY 18: Jaylin Stewart #3 of the Connecticut Huskies gets the rebound against Hudson Greer #10 of the Creighton Bluejays during the first half of an NCAA men's basketball game at Harry A. Gampel Pavilion on February 18, 2026 in Storrs, Connecticut. (Photo by Joe Buglewicz/Getty Images) Joe Bug

Let me now compile my analysis and make a decision. The data strongly favors Duke:

1. Duke is #1 in both KenPom and Torvik with a historic +39.17 efficiency margin
2. UConn has good but not elite metrics - 12th in SRS, 24th in defense, 54th in offense
3. Duke has the #1 defense and #4 offense
4. Duke has been dominant even without injured starters Caleb Foster and Patrick Ngongba II
5. UConn has been struggling lately - lost to Marquette and blown out by St. John's in the Big East tournament
6. The efficiency margin gap is enormous - likely 15-20+ points

UConn's credible upset path would need to rely on:
- Dan Hurley's championship pedigree
- Their 24th-ranked defense making enough stops
- Potential offensive explosion from players like Liam McNeeley or Alex Karaban
- Potential Duke injury impacts

But Duke's defense is elite (#1 nationally) and their offense is top-5. Even without Foster and Ngongba, they've shown dominant depth. The statistical gap is too large to justify picking UConn based on the available data.

I'll pick Duke with high confidence.
